Featured

  • Post

    Introducing Nozomio v1, the best search and index API for technical data.


    When I finished Y Combinator, I announced Nozomio as a standalone MCP server that gives agents up to date docs.

    A lot has changed since then.

    Watching how customers actually use the product made the real shape of the problem obvious. People are not just “reading docs”. They’re trying to give agents reliable context across everything technical: codebases, internal docs, PDFs, blog posts, datasets, tickets, changelogs, wikis, and increasingly, internal company knowledge.

    And the pattern became clear:

    Context is king for AI agents.

    Knowledge work has always been messy. Docs go stale. The real learnings live in tribal knowledge. Best practices spread through osmosis, not systems.

    AI agents flip this. The leverage you get from agents depends on how effectively you can give them the right data, structured correctly, kept current, and grounded in your workflows.

    That’s what Nozomio is for.

    We help you:

    - index technical sources (code, docs, PDFs, research, datasets, web, and more)
    - search with high precision and low hallucination risk
    - plug into agents so the “context layer” stays always on

    A small subset of what’s new in v1:

    1) Nia Slack Search API
    Slack search is frustratingly bad: mostly keyword-only, no semantic understanding, and painful when you need decisions from old threads. So we built a Slack Search API that indexes your workspace, keeps it synced in near real time, and lets you search semantically or via agentic retrieval.

    2) Tree Reasoning for PDFs
    Traditional RAG treats PDFs as bags of text: chunk, embed, hope. It breaks on complex docs where structure matters.

    We built structure-aware PDF search: we extract the document hierarchy, tag chunks by section/page, then have an LLM reason over the tree to decide where to look before running retrieval. When you ask about “results”, you get results.

    3) Agent-native integrations
    Nozomio works with the tools people actually use: plugins + skills that plug into any agent setup (Cursor, OpenClaw, Claude Code, custom agents, etc.). We’re also expanding data sources aggressively so the context layer can cover more of your stack.

    Under the hood, we’re betting heavily on research because this problem does not get solved by “just use web search”. The hard part is retrieval that stays correct as data scales, changes, and gets messy.

    Also, a quick update: since launch we’ve brought on Angela Chen, Timothee Cour, and Gokul Rajaram as strategic angels.

    If you’re building with agents (or trying to make them actually useful inside a team), I’d love to talk.

    884 · 104 comments

  • Post

    Announcing Nozomio's $6.2M round, led by CRV, BoxGroup, and LocalGlobe.


    I started Nozomio in high school six months ago as an AI agent for codebases that could cross-reference multiple repos, answer questions, and help developers onboard themselves. I failed.

    But I knew there was something in the agent space that every developer wants: the right context.

    Right before my Y Combinator batch, I was struggling to give coding agents the right context. They missed versions, filled up the context window, and used incorrect frameworks. That is when I built the first iteration of Nia, which is now an augmentation layer for AI agents.

    Context is the most important problem in the agent space. It prevents AI systems from achieving superhuman code intelligence, and I want to solve it.

    The current state of Nia might change a thousand times, but the problem will stay the same. Even as an MCP, which is very novel and underdeveloped, Nia has already improved every coding agent by at least 30%. I am on the mission to 100x it.

    This round was led by CRV, BoxGroup, and LocalGlobe, with participation from Y Combinator and 20VC Product. I am also backed by world-class angel investors, including:

    - Paul Graham (Founder of Y Combinator)
    - Joshua Schachter, Founder of del.icio.us (acq. by Yahoo) and Tasty Labs (acq. by Walmart)
    - Gustav Söderström (Co-President, CTO at Spotify)
    - Thomas Wolf (Co-founder of Hugging Face)
    - Richard Aberman (Founder of WePay (YC S09) and former YC Visiting Partner)
    - Michael Fertik (Founder of Reputation, Modelcode AI, Heroic VC)
    - Tomer Cohen (CPO at LinkedIn)
    - Jack Brody (CPO at Suno)
    - Geoff Charles (CPO at Ramp)
    - Sabrina Hahn
    - Kulveer Taggar, Founder of Zeus (YC S11) and Auctomatic (YC W07)
    - Chris Howard (Ritual Capital)

    Nozomio is also backed by people associated with OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Databricks, Airbnb, and Microsoft.

    I am hiring. I am solo right now and will stay under 20 employees until IPO. Apply if you are crazy enough.

    https://lnkd.in/e3wHCrXR

    1,901 · 291 comments

  • Post

    I got into YC S25 as a high-school dropout from Kazakhstan.


    I first heard about YC from my father when I was just a kid in elementary school. I can’t recall the exact conversation, but it happened during one of our occasional walks along the local river in Almaty, the city where I was born:

    “Arlan, there are several types of universities: some where people go just to get a degree, and others that are designed specifically for founders. There’s something called     Y Combinator, where founders pitch their ideas and, if accepted, receive funding and access to an incredible network to help build their companies. It’s one of the best places to be as an early-stage founder. One day, I want you to get in. It’s in the heart of Silicon Valley—where dreams are built.”

    I was really interested in entrepreneurship, but I didn’t pay much attention at the time because it was a bit overwhelming for me to fully grasp everything back then.

    As time passed, I began digging deeper into the roots of Silicon Valley, mostly by reading about Fairchild Semiconductor, Frederick Terman, and Steve Jobs.

    I became obsessed.

    Before starting Nozomio, I hacked on various projects, including Hanlan Tech, my first startup, which I launched at the age of 15. Although I managed to scale it to 20,000 users, I couldn’t see myself working on it ten years down the line. So I decided to let it go and bring it to an end. Looking back, it turned out to be an extremely valuable experience.

    A couple of years ago, my dad actually applied to YC with one of his startups, but it ultimately didn’t get in.

    It made one thing clear to me: I have to finish the work.

    After two failed attempts, I wasn’t discouraged. They only strengthened my motivation and showed my persistence.

    Before applying to the S25, I made one thing clear to myself: I’m not letting it go this time. I dropped out of high school and temporarily moved to London, where I raised my pre-seed round led by LocalGlobe and other incredible people. I wanted to make the most of my time there, so I focused on three things: talking to users, programming, and learning as much as I could from founders.

    A couple of weeks later, I received an interview invite. It gave me a boost of hope, especially since I hadn’t heard from YC in over a month and wasn’t expecting a response.

    The interview went smoothly but was extremely intense, as a founder has to convince several partners why their company could be the next rocket ship.

    The interview ended, and a couple of hours went by. Then, at exactly 12:16 AM, I got a call from an unknown number: “Hey Arlan, we would love to fund Nozomio for the upcoming summer batch.”

    I didn't sleep after.

    Still, YC is just the beginning for Nozomio. There are infinite possibilities ahead.

    Thank you for being part of my journey.

    - Arlan

    1,950 · 204 comments

  • Post

    I am thrilled to release the new version of Nia, a tool that gives coding agents the context they need.


    Over the past four months, I raised more than $1.8 million, dropped my high school degree, moved to SF, and got into     Y Combinator as a solo founder from Kazakhstan to build Nia.

    But one problem has followed me since the creation of GitHub Copilot: context.

    I’ve come to the conclusion that in order to achieve superhuman coding intelligence, we need to solve it. Developers love coding agents, but still rely on them to retrieve the right and up-to-date information, whether it’s open-source SDKs or Stripe documentation. The real bottleneck isn’t code generation; it’s the ability to not just understand your own codebase, but also to retrieve external context.

    I have built a novel approach to retrieve and process external context.
    I improved Cursor's accuracy by 30%.
    I used my tool to migrate entire services, all within hours.

    In the past month, users have already queried over 10TB of data through Nia and indexed more than 120,000 webpages. Most of my customers tell me their context gets cleaner and more precise every single day.

    I will continue learning by building, and my eventual goal is to completely change software engineering.

    I will build the future where people no longer use IDEs and English. Everyone should have the ability to create, just by thinking.

    1,061 · 243 comments

  • Post

    Introducing Nia MCP by Nozomio


    I am starting a new era where you never have to hunt down docs, repos, or specs again.

    I was kicked out of my math classes for taking customer calls, so I decided to drop out of high school, move to London, raise over $1M in a few days, and then got into Y Combinator as a solo founder from Kazakhstan who wants to change the world.

    Besides that, I’ve seen the struggle firsthand: your coding agent can generate code beautifully, but it still can’t find the right context.

    Nia will transform the way you feed context to ANY coding agent.

    - Bulk-index any number of repos & doc sites in one MCP call.
    - Deep-Research Agent for any topic on software engineering that can pass information back to Nia’s storage tool for long-term use.
    - Plug-and-play with Cursor, VS Code, JetBrains, or any MCP-compatible IDE.

    No more ten tabs, no more copy-paste. It lets your agent ship faster and smarter.

    It is live as v0:
    • Grab your free API key (25 reqs) → https://trynia.ai/
    • Install in 5 sec: uvx nia-mcp-server (or pip install nia-mcp-server)
    • Drop in your mcs.json, and watch your agent level-up

    513 · 46 comments

    Introducing Demo Night, brought to you by Nozomio and Scale Venture Partners.


    Friday night in SF. Builders only.

    bring what you’re working on
    dide project, early prototype, or something you shipped last week

    we’re putting founders in a room with one goal: show what you’ve built and get real feedback with no panels and corporate bs.

    we are going to have food, drinks, and a great company.
    apr 10, 6–9pm, sf

    want to demo? mention it in your rsvp

  • Arlan Rakhmetzhanov

    • 2nd

    hiring | founder @ nozomio (yc s25), hs dropout, ex stanford research

    5d •

    I require every employee at my company to doomscroll for at least 30 minutes a day.


    Reels. TikTok. X. Reddit.

    Most people think this is brainrot.

    They are wrong. I think it is market research.

    If you are building for the next generation of consumers, founders, or companies, you cannot afford to be disconnected from what people are paying attention to right now.

    Amjad Masad from Replit said it well recently: “being extremely online is not necessarily a vice. It can be an advantage.”

    You see what people want on the internet before they know how to explain it properly.

    You see behavior shift there first.
    You see language change there first.
    You see new distribution there first.

    Especially in AI, everything moves too fast to rely only on reports, podcasts, or secondhand opinions. Why do you think so many people are switching to TBPN instead of relying on legacy media channels that are stuck in the 2000s?

    You need to be close to the feed and see what people are obsessed with, joking about, buying into, and getting tired of (a lot of B2B companies and dev tools run ads on instagram reels).

    A lot of what people dismiss as brainrot is actually raw signal in an unserious format.

    Being terminally online is only bad if you do nothing with it. Used correctly, it becomes taste.

    It becomes an unfair advantage and instinct. You should scroll more.

    134 reactions134

    19 comments19 comments

    5d •

    Introducing a public Nozomio skill to test Nia on its most popular use case: providing context to coding agents.

    It’s a simple skill I built that doesn’t require an API key to get started, so you can try Nia before creating an account.

    Run 'npx skills add nozomio-labs/nia-public-skill-demo' in your terminal.

      • Arlan Rakhmetzhanov

        • 2nd

        hiring | founder @ nozomio (yc s25), hs dropout, ex stanford research

        1w •

        Nozomio Labs is hiring, and we are staying under 30 people until IPO. We are a research and product lab solving context in the AI era, starting with Nia, a search and indexing API for AI agents. I raised $6 million as a solo high school founder, and now Nozomio is finally hiring. I value agency above anything else, and recently convinced a guy to drop out of NYU after 1 day of meeting him. That is the kind of person I want around me. We’re hiring a couple of founding engineers, researchers, and growth people who are insanely high-agency and want to build something massive. I am only bringing on 2 to 5 people, so this will be the most intense and competitive time of your life, surrounded by some of the best and smartest people on earth, in the heart of San Francisco. I do not care much about credentials. I care whether you are an artist, maker, or an opinionated underdog who does not follow societal norms. Bonus if you are extremely funny, terminally online, and spend thousands of dollars on coding agents. I will personally give you a company credit card to pay for all API credits. If that sounds like you, comment your email address and repost, and I will fly you out to San Francisco to build with me.

            • 2nd

            hiring | founder @ nozomio (yc s25), hs dropout, ex stanford research

            1w •

            The time has come. Introducing Nozomio hackathon.


            api and cli products are dominating tech right now, so we’re going all in.

            Nozomio is hosting a sf-based api / cli hackathon (may 2–3) and we’re making it one of the biggest in the space.

            we’re expecting 200–400 top builders working on api-first and cli-native products, with real distribution across dev communities.

            we’re currently bringing on paid sponsors.

            prizes will include:
            - cash
            - A LOT of credits
            - macbooks
            and other high-signal rewards

            if you’re an api-first company and want distribution + real usage (not just impressions), this is a strong channel.

            dm me if you want to sponsor or have ideas for prizes.

          • Arlan Rakhmetzhanov

            • 2nd

            hiring | founder @ nozomio (yc s25), hs dropout, ex stanford research

            1w • Edited •

            you DON’T need a lot of money or decades of experience to start a global startup.


            one of the biggest misconceptions people have is that you first need to build some local cash machine, get rich, and only then start working on something ambitious. i do not think that is true at all.

            if your goal is to build something global, optimize for being in the right environment and maximizing your speed of learning, not just maximizing your initial capital.

            people massively overestimate how much money you need and underestimate how much leverage you can get from being high-agency, technical, and deeply obsessed with users.

            elon musk moved from south africa to canada at 17 with very little money, and that decision ended up changing the trajectory of his life. later, he finished his studies at the university of pennsylvania and got experience in the united states. the point is not that everyone should copy his path exactly.

            the point is that you do not need perfect conditions to start moving.

            this is also why startup culture is so different across countries. in a lot of asian countries, including kazakhstan, korea, and japan, young people are pushed toward stability from the beginning.

            1. kids are pushed into tutoring before they even reach high school
            2. parents pressure them to become consultants, doctors, or follow other traditional careers
            3. they are taught that a stable life with a decent income is the safest and best outcome
            4. in places like korea, large family-controlled companies dominate the economy and concentrate access, resources, and opportunity at the top
            when that is the default culture, risk-taking becomes abnormal. ambition gets filtered out early. people start optimizing for approval instead of upside.

            my view is simple: you do not need a huge amount of money or experience to move to the states or any other country and start building. what matters more is how badly you want it, how much uncertainty you can tolerate, and whether you are willing to put yourself on the line.

            i know a guy (            Bereket Engida) who grew up in ethiopia, became a solo founder, got into yc within months, built better auth, and raised millions.

            stories like that happen because high-agency people do not wait for ideal circumstances. they create momentum with whatever they have.

            if you cannot move yet, start building anyway. use the internet, talk to users, ship products, and get momentum.

            but do not convince yourself that you need to get rich first before you are allowed to think globally.

            195 reactions195

            17 comments17 comments

            •

          • Arlan Rakhmetzhanov

            • 2nd

            hiring | founder @ nozomio (yc s25), hs dropout, ex stanford research

            1w • Edited •

            We're excited to announce the Nozomio + LangChain integration.


            Nia is a search and index API that gives AI agents reliable, up-to-date context from documentation, codebases, research papers, datasets, and more.

            With this integration, any LangChain agent gets access to 20 tools: search, source management, GitHub code exploration, cross-agent context sharing, and dependency analysis. All with full async support.

            Available in both Python and TypeScript:

            - pip install langchain-nia
            - npm install @nozomioai/langchain-nia

            Check it out: https://lnkd.in/ghfRZyaD

              • Arlan Rakhmetzhanov

                • 2nd

                hiring | founder @ nozomio (yc s25), hs dropout, ex stanford research

                2w •

                I am a high schooler who has been running a $50M+ startup completely solo for almost a year. Here is what I learned:


                Disclaimer: thanks to AI, it is more than possible to build a unicorn with a 2-3 person team, but it is like playing Minecraft on ultra-hardcore mode with 3 hearts instead of 10. It will stress you out, put you through agony, and force discipline on you like nothing else.

                First, why would I even run a startup alone?

                For context, I dropped out of 11th grade from pretty much third-world country, and my school was still figuring out how to use GPT-3.5 while I was shipping 5k+ lines of code per day during class.

                90% of people around me did not even know what a startup was and were more interested in traditional career paths like investment banking or nursing. Ever since I was little, I wanted to take the biggest risks possible, so building a company felt like the obvious path.

                Since no one around me cared about building something huge, I dropped out, pursued my dream of becoming a Silicon Valley entrepreneur, and flew to SF for                 Y Combinator Summer 2025. Here I am today, still in the trenches, but in the funniest and most intense period of my life.

                1) You become really good at multitasking.

                When you are solo, you handle everything at once: sales calls, bug fixes, and talking to users. I set up automations to route support tickets to my phone, bought extra monitors to keep Slack and Twitter open 24/7, and coded in Ubers.

                It gets really chaotic after a couple of weeks, and I would not recommend this path to most people. But over time, it sharpens your critical thinking and teaches you how to prioritize under pressure and break down tasks faster.

                2) You have to be terminally online.

                I am known for replying at AI speed. Not because I use AI to reply to everyone, but because I am addicted to my phone and obsessed with customers.
                When a user messages me, I reply in 10 seconds and try to fix things immediately, even if I am on the toilet. In the early days, iteration speed and how fast the founder fixes bugs matter more than polish. Customers usually respect that.

                3) AI 10x’s you as a founder, engineer, designer, and more.

                As a solo founder, you learn very quickly how to use it well. You notice nitpicks others miss, stay glued to Twitter to keep up with AI research, and adopt tools as early as possible.

                I was one of the first people obsessing over file systems and researching how agents should fundamentally interact with humans. Combine laziness with AI addiction, and it becomes a ridiculous productivity weapon. Leveraged properly, it can compress years of learning into months.

                There is definitely more I would love to share, but these were the first things that came to mind. Thanks for reading.

                P.S. I am hiring founding team members: growth interns, researchers, and cracked engineers. DM me!

                336 reactions336

                31 comments31 comments
